name: Roman Camp of Petavonium from Castrocontrigo - circular by the Eria River
name_original: Campamento Romano de Petavonium desde Castrocontrigo – circular por el río Eria
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Castrocontrigo traverse the Valdería region, characterized by diverse landscapes. The area features moderately challenging road rides with varied terrain, including climbs and well-paved sections, though some routes may include unpaved segments. The Eria River is a prominent natural feature, with routes often passing through its vicinity. The region also includes scenic elements like the Tabla Reservoir and evergreen oak forests. Best touring cycling routes around Castrocontrigo The most popular touring cycling route is Roman Camp of Petavonium from Castrocontrigo - circular by the Eria River, a 46.2 miles (74.4 km) trail that takes 5 hours 6 minutes to complete. This route is classified as difficult and follows the Eria River. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Church of Castrocontrigo loop from Castrocontrigo de la Valdería, a difficult 26.5 miles (42.7 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain, reaching over 2,600 feet (800 meters) of ascent. Local touring cyclists also love the Vidriales Valley and Petavonium Camp Trail, a 22.6 miles (36.3 km) trail leading through the Vidriales Valley, often completed in about 2 hours 39 minutes. Touring cycling around Castrocontrigo is defined by varied terrain, the Eria River, and evergreen oak forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging routes with substantial climbs. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 20 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Castrocontrigo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Castrocontrigo?
A: There are 6 touring cycling routes around Castrocontrigo, offering a variety of experiences for different fitness levels. These include 3 moderate and 3 difficult routes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Castrocontrigo?
A: The routes around Castrocontrigo feature diverse terrain, from moderately challenging road rides with comfortable gradients to sections that may include unpaved segments. You'll encounter scenic elements like the Eria River, the Tabla Reservoir, and evergreen oak forests. Some routes, such as the 5 km ascent from Villalverde towards Castrocontrigo hill, offer spectacular views.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, Castrocontrigo offers several circular touring cycling routes. A notable one is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037602481/roman-camp-of-petavonium-from-castrocontrigo-circular-by-the-eria-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Camp of Petavonium from Castrocontrigo - circular by the Eria River&lt;/a&gt;, which is 46.2 miles (74.4 km) long. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10461664" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Castrocontrigo loop from Castrocontrigo de la Valdería&lt;/a&gt;, a challenging 26.5 miles (42.7 km) path with significant elevation gain.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for families or less experienced cyclists?
A: While there are no routes specifically classified as 'easy' in our current selection, there are 3 moderate routes that might be suitable for families or less experienced cyclists with good basic fitness. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383958335/vidriales-valley-and-petavonium-camp-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vidriales Valley and Petavonium Camp Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 22.6 miles (36.3 km) route that can be completed in about 2 hours 39 minutes.

Q: What is the most challenging touring cycling route in Castrocontrigo?
A: For those seeking a significant challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10461664" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Castrocontrigo loop from Castrocontrigo de la Valdería&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 26.5 miles (42.7 km) route featuring over 2,600 feet (800 meters) of ascent, requiring very good fitness.

Q: What are some interesting places to see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Along the routes, you can explore cultural and natural landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037602481/roman-camp-of-petavonium-from-castrocontrigo-circular-by-the-eria-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Camp of Petavonium&lt;/a&gt; is a significant historical site. You might also pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5452845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Castrocontrigo&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6039414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castrocontrigo Castle&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5470465" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Eria Bridge in Castrocalbón&lt;/a&gt;. The region's rich Mediterranean flora and fauna, including evergreen oak forests, also offer beautiful scenery.

Q: Are there any viewpoints or scenic spots on the cycling routes?
A: Yes, many routes offer picturesque views. The climb from Villalverde towards Castrocontrigo hill is known for spectacular views of the surrounding landscape. Additionally, routes passing by the Eria River and the Tabla Reservoir provide beautiful natural vistas.

Q: What is the longest touring cycling route in Castrocontrigo?
A: The longest touring cycling route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037602481/roman-camp-of-petavonium-from-castrocontrigo-circular-by-the-eria-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Camp of Petavonium from Castrocontrigo - circular by the Eria River&lt;/a&gt;, covering 46.2 miles (74.4 km) and typically taking around 5 hours and 6 minutes to complete.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Castrocontrigo?
A: The Valdería region, where Castrocontrigo is located, falls within the Mediterranean phytogeographical area. This means summers can be arid. Spring and autumn generally offer more temperate weather conditions, making them ideal for touring cycling. Always check the local weather forecast before your ride.

Q: What are some natural features to look out for while cycling?
A: The Eria River is a prominent natural feature, with several routes passing through its vicinity. The river is also a trout fishing reserve, indicating clear waters and a healthy ecosystem. You'll also encounter the Tabla Reservoir and diverse Mediterranean flora, including evergreen oak forests. Keep an eye out for wildlife such as deer, roe deer, wild boars, and foxes.

Q: Are there any other outdoor activities besides cycling in the Castrocontrigo area?
A: Beyond cycling, the region is also suitable for hiking, with various trails available to explore the landscape. The clear waters of the Eria River provide opportunities for fishing, particularly for native trout. The broader Castilla y León region also offers activities like kayaking and paddle surfing, reflecting the area's active tourism spirit.

Q: What is the typical duration for touring cycling routes in Castrocontrigo?
A: The duration of routes varies depending on their length and difficulty. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037602481/roman-camp-of-petavonium-from-castrocontrigo-circular-by-the-eria-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Camp of Petavonium from Castrocontrigo - circular by the Eria River&lt;/a&gt; takes approximately 5 hours and 6 minutes, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383958335/vidriales-valley-and-petavonium-camp-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vidriales Valley and Petavonium Camp Trail&lt;/a&gt; can be completed in about 2 hours and 39 minutes.
tips: An archaeological site ruins the Hermitage and the Palace located in the valley of the Eria river. Few remains of the palace apart from a restored Gothic arch.

There are several theories about the peculiar name of this Castro: some suggest that it comes from the lime that was manufactured here; others if it was founded by a member of the Calvo Roman family; It is also thought that it may be due to the oak-based charcoal that was produced in its vicinity, and there are those who think that the origin of the name is, on the contrary, Mozarabic.

An archaeological site of great importance, the Roman Camp of Petavonium is a vestige of the Roman Empire in the Iberian Peninsula. The camp was a fortified outpost protecting trade routes in the area.
Nice climb towards Castrocontrigo from Quintanilla along the ZA-125 road surrounded by pine forests.

Although the current name of this town is much clearer than that of its neighbor Castrocalbón, it is interesting to know that the origin of Castrocontrigo is in the primitive city of Aria, which was the one that gave its name to the Aria/Eria river, which crosses the municipality. , and in turn to the entire Valle del Aria, which ended up being known as Valdería.
